== Introduction to incremental reading ==

Traditional linear reading is highly inefficient. This comes from the fact that various pieces of the text are of various importance. Some should be skipped. Others should be read in the first order of priority. Old-fashioned books are quickly being replaced with hypertext. Hypertext will help you quickly jump to information that is the most important at any given moment. Hypertext requires a different style of writing. All linear texts can assume that the reader is familiar with the preceding sections. This makes them context-poor. Within hypertext, individual texts become context-independent, and all difficult terms and concepts are explained primarily with additional hyperlinks. In the same way in which the web helped delinearize the global sources of information, SuperMemo can help you delinearize your reading of whatever linear material you decide to import to SuperMemo. While reading with SuperMemo, you will see a linear text as a sequence of sections subdivided into paragraphs and individual sentences. SuperMemo will help you provide a separate and independent processing for each section, paragraph or sentence.

== What is incremental reading? ==

Incremental reading is a learning technique that makes it possible to [[Incremental learning#Can you really read thousands of articles at the same time?|read thousands of articles at the same time]] without getting lost. Incremental reading begins with importing articles from electronic sources, e.g. the Internet. The student then extracts the most important fragments of individual articles for further review. Extracted fragments are then converted into questions and answers. These in turn become subject to systematic review and repetition that maximizes the long-term recall. The review process is handled by the proven ''[http://super-memory.com/english/ol/background.htm spaced repetition algorithm]'' known as the [[SuperMemo|'''SuperMemo method''']].

<div class="bs-callout bs-callout-warning">
'''Incremental reading converts electronic articles into durable knowledge in your memory.''' This conversion requires minimum keyboard&mouse work:

* '''Input''': electronic articles (e.g. collected from the net)
* '''Output''': well-remembered knowledge (quizzed regularly in the form of questions and answers)
</div>

In incremental reading, you read articles in small portions. After you read a portion of one article, you go on to a portion of another article, etc. You introduce all important portions of texts into the learning process in SuperMemo. This way you do not worry that you forget the main thread of the article, even if you return to reading months later. Your progress with individual articles may be slow, but you greatly increase your efficiency by paying less attention to less important articles and spending more time on articles that are more beneficial to your knowledge. Difficult articles may wait until you read easier explanatory articles, etc. Last but not least, incremental reading increases your efficiency because it is fun! You never get bored. If you do not like an article, you read just a sentence and jump to other articles. This way your attention and focus stay maximized.

'''Warning!''' Incremental reading may seem complex at first. However, once you master it, you will begin a learning process that will surpass your expectations. You will be surprised with the volume of data your memory can process and retain! See this [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DoQoeK53bP8 simple demo at YouTube].

== Five basic skills of incremental reading ==

Incremental reading requires skills that you will perfect only over months and years of use. This overview will only help you master the basic skills and help you make a start with incremental reading. The 5 basic skills are:

* [[#Skill 1: Importing articles|importing articles]] to [[SuperMemo]]
* [[#Skill 2: Reading articles|reading articles]] and decomposing articles into manageable pieces
* [[#Skill 3: Extracting fragments, questions and answers|converting most important pieces of knowledge into question-answer material]]
* [[#Skill 4: Repetition and review|review of the material]] to ensure good recall
* [[#Skill 5: Handling large volumes of knowledge|handling of the unavoidable overflow of information]]

=== Skill 1: Importing articles ===

==== Five article import methods ====

Initially, you may limit your imports to a simple copy&paste of individual articles. Later, you will want to master automatic imports from the web that offer many advantages.

Here are the 5 main article import methods in SuperMemo:

* '''[[#Import by Copy&Paste|Copy&Paste]]''': select a text of an article in the browser (or any other application that allows of copying texts), copy it to the clipboard, and copy it to SuperMemo with a single keystroke: ''Ctrl+N''
* '''[[#Import of multiple articles|Mass import]]''': use a dedicated [[Web import|web import option]] to import many articles from supported browser. This method allows of avoiding duplicate imports, marks your imports with [[references]], imports only selected portions of texts, and offers many other advantages.
* '''[[#dedicated imports|Dedicated imports]]''': SuperMemo makes it particularly easy to import material from [http://en.wikipedia.org Wikipedia] (the recommended source of basic incremental reading materials) and from [http://youtube.com YouTube] (a source of [[Glossary:Incremental_video|incremental video]] materials)
* '''[[#Importing articles from local files|Local file imports]]''': import files that you have already collected on your hard disk
* '''[[Incremental mail processing#Importing mail to SuperMemo|Mail imports]]''': for incremental processing of your mail

===== Import by Copy&Paste =====

To import an article with copy and paste, follow these steps:

* Select the imported text in your web browser and copy the selection to the clipboard (e.g. with ''Ctrl+C'')
* Switch to SuperMemo (e.g. with ''Alt+Tab'')
* In SuperMemo, press ''Ctrl+N'' (this is equivalent to '''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Edit menu#Add a new article|Add a new article]]''' on the [[main menu]]). SuperMemo will create a new [[Glossary:Element|element]], and paste the article. You can also use the '''Paste an article''' button ([[Image:Paste button.gif|SuperMemo: Paste article (from the clipboard) button on the learnbar]]) on the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]]
* Optionally, use ''Alt+P'' to define [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] of the imported article. Use the '''Percent''' field and remember that <code>0%</code> is the highest [[Glossary:Priority|priority]], while <code>100%</code> is the lowest [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]
* Optionally, use ''Ctrl+J'' to specify the first review [[Glossary:Interval|interval]]. For example: one day for high priority material or 30 days for low priority material

Please remember that if you have many articles opened in Internet Explorer, you can most easily import them with [[web import]] as described in the next section.

===== Import of multiple articles =====

The most convenient way to import learning materials to [[SuperMemo]] is a direct import of multiple pages right from the web. To import many pages at the same time, (1) open them in the [[web browser]], and (2) click the '''Import''' button on the [[learnbar]] (or press ''Shift+F8'', or chose '''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Web import]]''' on the [[main menu]]). To avoid importing advertising and other garbage, in the web browser, select portions of the text that is to be imported or choose the '''Parse''' option. If you select texts before imports, you are less likely to need filters to get rid of troublesome HTML (available by pressing ''F6'' on the imported article). If your browser is not supported, you will need to use [[#Import by Copy&Paste|Copy&Paste method]], or re-open the selected articles in a [[web browser|supported web browser]] (e.g. Microsoft Edge). 

For more details on importing materials from the web see: [[Web import]]

===== <span id="dedicated_imports">Dedicated imports</span> (Wikipedia, YouTube, and pictures) =====

The most popular sources of learning materials are [http://en.wikipedia.org Wikipedia] (for incremental reading) and [http://youtube.com YouTube] (for [[Glossary:Incremental_video|incremental video]]). You can quickly select those sources (as well as others) using the '''Sources''' toolbar options at the top of the [[Web import|'''Web import''' dialog box]]. Additionally, a dedicated filter will best prepare the imported pages for convenient learning.

<div class="center">
[[Image:Incremental reading Topic.jpg|800px|SuperMemo: A topic with an article about the greenhouse effect imported from Wikipedia]] 
{{:Image:Incremental reading Topic.jpg}}
</div>

If you want to import pictures, you can also use a picture quick-selection button that will ignore all non-picture pages opened in a supported [[web browser]]. Click '''Sources : Pictures''' in the [[Web import|'''Web import''' dialog]] when importing pages (with ''Shift+F8'').

For more details see: '''[[Web import]]'''

===== Importing articles from local files =====

If you want to import articles from files that reside on your local drive, you can use the following methods:

* '''Single article from Internet Explorer into a new [[Glossary:Element|element]]'''
*# open the local article in Internet Explorer
*# import in the same way as you import articles from the web (e.g. [[#Import by Copy&Paste|Import by Copy&Paste]] or [[#Import of multiple articles|Import of multiple articles]])
* '''Single article into existing [[HTML component]]'''
*# choose '''[[Component menu#File|File]] : Import file''' on the HTML [[component menu]]
* '''Multiple articles (stored in a single folder)'''
*# use '''[[File menu|File]] : [[File menu#Import|Import]] : Files and folders'''
*# point to the folder that contains the articles (make sure no other files are stored in the folder or its subfolders, otherwise, they will also get imported to SuperMemo)

=== Skill 2: Reading articles ===

Here is a simplified algorithm for reading articles:

# '''Choose an article''': Import an article as [[#Skill 1: Importing articles|explained earlier]] or bring up previously imported articles with '''[[Learn]]''' (''Ctrl+L''). '''[[Learn]]''' will display only articles imported in the past. If you import an article, and want to have it shown later during a learning session on the same day, you must place it in the [[Glossary:Outstanding queue|outstanding queue]] (e.g. '''[[Element menu#Learning|Learning]] : Later today''' on the [[element menu]], ''Ctrl+Shift+J'', etc.). If you import many articles that you want to process on the same day, you must place them all in the [[Glossary:Outstanding queue|outstanding queue]]. For example, open the articles in the [[browser]], and choose '''[[Subset operations#Learning|Learning]] : Add all to outstanding''' (or use '''Add to outstanding''' icon on the [[browser toolbar]]). Most of the time you can rely solely on '''[[Learn]]''' to schedule articles optimally for review.
# '''Click the article''' to enter the [[Glossary:Editing mode|editing mode]], in which you can modify text, select fragments, etc. Optionally, use filter (''F6''), if the text is hard to process (e.g. selections are hard, [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] not marked correctly, etc.)
# '''Start reading''' the article from the top or from the last [[Glossary:Read-point|read point]]
# '''Extract texts''': If you encounter an interesting text in the article, select it and choose '''[[Learnbar#Extract|Remember extract]]''' on the [[learnbar]] (or press ''Alt+X''). This operation will introduce the extracted fragment into the learning process as an independent mini-article. If you would like to specify the [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] of the new [[Glossary:Extract|extract]], choose '''[[Component menu#Reading|Reading]] : Schedule extract''' ([[File:Schedule extract.png|Reading : Schedule extract, available on the Read toolbar, enables you to specify the priority of a text fragment being extracted]]) instead of '''Remember extract'''. Also, if you have an impression that the article is difficult and you would like to read some fragments later, extract those fragments with '''[[Component menu#Reading|Reading]] : Schedule extract''' and provide a review [[Glossary:Interval|interval]] that will reflect the time you believe you will be better equipped to understand the extracted fragment.
# Optionally, use '''Delete before cursor''' (''Alt+\''). This will delete the text that you have read, clean up the article, remove garbage, and help tackle HTML that is difficult to process. 
# Optionally, if you read a fragment that seems unimportant, select it (e.g. with the mouse) and either delete it (e.g. with the ''Del'' key) or mark it with the <span class="ignore">ignore</span> style. To mark a text as <span class="ignore">ignore</span>, choose '''[[Component menu#Reading|Reading]] : Ignore''' on the [[component menu]], click the '''Ignore text''' button ([[File:Ignore.png|Reading : Ignore makes it possible for you to mark an unimportant fragment with the ignore style]]) on the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]], or just press ''Ctrl+Shift+I''.
# Optionally, if the selected fragment does not include all the important reading context, you can add this context manually. For example, if you are learning history, you may extract the following fragment from an article about Lincoln: ''On Sept. 22, 1862, President Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation, one of the most important messages in the history of the world. He signed it Jan. 1, 1863.'' If you would like to extract the fragment related to signing the Emancipation Proclamation, you will need to change ''He'' to ''Lincoln'' and ''it'' to ''Emancipation Proclamation'' so that your stand-alone fragment is understandable: ''Lincoln signed the Emancipation Proclamation on Jan. 1, 1863.'' You can use the '''[[Component menu#Reference|Reference]]''' options on the [[component menu]] to easily add context to your [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] (see: [[#Adding references|References]]). Context added by '''[[Component menu#Reference|Reference]]''' will be added automatically to all [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] of a given article. For example, select the text that you want to serve as the reference title of all [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and choose '''[[Component menu#Reference|Reference]] : Title''' on the HTML [[component menu]] (or press ''Alt+T''). This text will appear at the bottom of all [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] (in <span class="reference">reference pink font</span> by default).
# Optionally, mark your last [[Glossary:Read-point|read point]]: Once you decide to stop reading the article before its end, mark the last processed fragment as the [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]] (e.g. with <i>Ctrl+F7</i> or by choosing '''[[Component menu#Reading|Reading]] : [[Component menu#Read-points|Read-points]] : Set read-point''' from the HTML [[component menu]]). Next time you come back to this same article, SuperMemo will highlight your [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]] and you will be able to resume reading from the point you last stopped reading the article. To go to your current [[Glossary:Read-point|read point]], press ''Alt+F7''. If you forget to set a [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]], SuperMemo will leave a [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]] at the place of your last [[Glossary:Extract|extract]] or last highlight.
# '''Go to the next article''': After you finish reading a portion of one article, choose '''[[Learn]]''' or '''Next repetition''' to proceed with reading other articles. Those buttons are located at the bottom of the [[element window]]. You can also use ''Enter'', which will work as long as the selection in the text is not empty (e.g. marked as a [[Glossary:Read-point|reading point]]), or if you have left the [[Glossary:Editing_mode|editing mode]] (e.g. with ''Esc''). If no text is selected, ''Enter'' will add a new line in the text (as is the case with standard text editors).
# Optionally, determine the next review date (e.g. with ''Ctrl+Shift+R''), or set the new [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] for the article (e.g. with ''Alt+P'').
# '''In incremental reading, interrupted reading is a rule, not an exception!''' With a dose of practice, you will quickly get accustomed to this not-so-natural state of affairs and learn to appreciate the power of the incremental approach. [[Incremental learning#The value of interruption in learning|The main role of interruption is to prevent the decline in the quality of reading]]. Use the following criteria to decide when to stop reading the article:
#* '''lack of time''': if you still have many articles for review for a given day and your time is running out, keep your increments shorter. After some time, being in a hurry will be a norm and you will tend to read only 1-2 paragraphs of each article and dig deeper only into groundbreaking articles that will powerfully affect your knowledge.
#* '''boredom''': if the article tends to make you bored, stop reading. Your attention span is always limited. If your focus is poor, you will benefit more from the article if you return to it after some break. Go on to reading something that you are not yet tired of. If SuperMemo schedules the next review at a date you consider too late, use ''Ctrl+J'' or ''Shift+Ctrl+R'' to adjust the next review date.
#* '''lack of understanding''': if you feel you need more knowledge before you are able to understand the article, postpone it (e.g. use ''Ctrl+J'' or ''Shift+Ctrl+R'' and schedule the next review in 100 days or so). If you believe you have already imported articles with relevant explanatory knowledge, you can search for these articles (e.g. with ''Ctrl+F''). Once you find them, you can (1) execute a [[subset review]], or (2) add the articles to the [[Glossary:Outstanding_queue|outstanding queue]] for reading on the same day, or (3) advance the articles (for example, in the [[browser]], you can execute: '''[[Subset operations#Learning|Learning]] : Review all''', or '''[[Subset operations#Learning|Learning]] : Add to outstanding''', or '''[[Subset operations#Advance|Advance]] : Topics'''). If you have not yet imported any explanatory articles, you can do it now (e.g. search the web and [[#Skill 1: Importing articles|import articles]] as explained before). Note that you can select a piece of text in SuperMemo and use ''Ctrl+F3'' to search encyclopedias or dictionaries for more material on a given subject.
#* '''lower priority''': read lower priority articles in smaller portions thus reducing the overall time allocation to low priority subjects.
#* '''overload''': if you have a long queue of articles to read, you will naturally read in smaller portions
# Once you complete reading the entire article and have extracted all the interesting fragments, choose '''[[Learnbar#Done!|Done!]]''' ([[File:Done!.png|Learning : Done will remove the article from the review process, and delete its contents (without deleting the extracted material]]) on the [[learnbar]]. You can also press ''Shift+Ctrl+Enter'', choose '''Done!''' in the [[SuperMemo Commander|Commander]], or choose '''[[Element menu#Learning|Learning]] : Done''' on the [[element menu]]. '''Done!''' will [[Glossary:Dismiss|dismiss]] the article and delete its contents (without deleting the extracted material). '''Done''' will delete a childless article (i.e. an article that did not provide any interesting [[Glossary:Extract|extract]]). Using '''Done''' will greatly reduce the size of your [[Glossary:Collection|collection]] and eliminate &quot;dead hits&quot; when [[Find elements|searching]] for texts.

<div class="center">
[[File:Read toolbar.jpg|800px|SuperMemo: The Read toolbar at the bottom of the element window. It features options used in incremental reading]]
{{:File:Read toolbar.jpg}}
</div>

=== Skill 3: Extracting fragments, questions and answers ===

==== Extracting texts ====

In the course of traditional reading, we often mark important paragraphs with a highlighter pen. In SuperMemo, those paragraphs can be extracted as separate mini-articles that will later be used to refresh your memory. Each extracted paragraph or section becomes a new [[Glossary:Element|element]] that will be subject to the same reading algorithm as the original article. Extract important fragments and single sentences with '''[[Extract]]''' ([[Image:Extract button.png|SuperMemo: Extract button on the learnbar in the element window]]). You can use ''Alt+X'', '''Extract''' on the [[learnbar]], or '''Reading : Remember extract''' on the [[component menu]].

==== Adding references ====

In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], you always need to quickly recover the context of a question or a piece of text. The easiest way to recover context quickly is via [[references]]. References propagate from [[Glossary:Element|element]] to element as you produce [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]. With all [[Glossary:Child|child elements]] produced from a given text marked with references, you would never need to worry about losing the context of the question. When you import from the web, references are added automatically. You can also define them manually field by field. Exemplary references are shown in pink in the picture below. For details see: [[References]].

<div class="center">
[[Image:Incremental reading Extract.jpg|800px|SuperMemo: An extract produced from an article about the greenhouse effect (references (in pink) at the bottom are added automatically)]] 
<blockquote>'''''Figure''': Typical snapshot of [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]]. While learning about the greenhouse effect, the student extracts the fragment saying that "In the absence of the greenhouse effect and an atmosphere, the Earth's average surface temperature of 14&nbsp;&deg;C (57&nbsp;&deg;F) could be as low as -18&nbsp;&deg;C (-0.4&nbsp;&deg;F), the black body temperature of the Earth.". The extracted fragment will inherit illustrations placed on the right, as well as article references. The student can move on to reading another article by pressing Enter. The picture on the right is stored locally in the image [[Glossary:Registry|registry]] (on the user's hard disk) and can be reused to illustrate other articles or questions.''</blockquote></div>

==== Cloze: Generating questions ====

SuperMemo will show you that extracting important fragments and reviewing them at later time will have an excellent impact on your ability to remember. However, it will also show that once the time between reviews
increases beyond 200-300 days, reading and re-reading ([[Glossary:Passive_review|passive review]]) will often result in insufficient recall. For this reason, sooner or later, you will need to convert your texts to specific questions. For that purpose you will use '''cloze deletion'''.

A cloze deletion is an [[Glossary:Item|item]] that uses an ellipsis (<span class="cloze">[...]</span>) to replace a part of a sentence.

For example:
<blockquote>
'''''Question''': The capital of Sierra Leone is <span class="cloze">[...]</span><br>
'''Answer''': Freetown''
</blockquote>

In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] are generated from [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] that have a form of a sentence or a simple paragraph.

To create a [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletion]] do the following:
# make sure a [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] contains a short sentence only (e.g. ''The capital of Sierra Leone is Freetown'')
# select an important keyword in that sentence (e.g. ''Freetown'')
# do one of the following:
#* click the '''Cloze''' button ([[File:Remember cloze.gif|Reading : Remember cloze uses the currently selected text to create a cloze deletion and memorize it]]) on the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]], or
#* press ''Alt+Z'', or
#* choose '''[[Component menu#Reading|Reading]] : Remember cloze''' from the HTML [[component menu]].

'''Remember cloze''' will convert a sentence into a specific question with an answer.  By using cloze, you will move from [[Glossary:Passive_review|passive review]] to [[Glossary:Active_recall|active recall]]. You do not need to wait until a paragraph or a sentence becomes hard to recall in [[Glossary:Passive_review|passive review]]. For your most important material, you can create [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze items]] immediately after finding a piece of information that you need to remember well.

The examples below show how to effectively use '''Remember cloze'''.

<div class="center">
[[File:Incremental_reading_Extract2.jpg|800px|Two numbers from the extracted sentence are used as keywords for generating questions and answers (temperatures of 14 °C and -18 °C)]]
<blockquote>'''''Figure''': Two numbers from the extracted sentence are used as keywords for generating questions and answers (temperatures of 14 °C and -18 °C)''</blockquote>

[[File:Incremental_reading_Cloze_deletion.jpg|800px|A question-answer item (in the form of a cloze deletion) forming the final product of incremental reading used in strengthening the memory of a given fact (here: hypothetical temperature on Earth devoid of atmosphere)]]
<blockquote>'''''Figure''': The sentence extracted during [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]] (see the previous picture) is converted into a [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletion]]. (i.e. a question-answer pair forming the final product of incremental reading used in strengthening the memory of a given fact (here: hypothetical temperature on Earth devoid of atmosphere)). The picture from the original [[Glossary:Extract|extract]] has been inherited (on the right). <span class="reference">Pink</span> texts at the bottom of the question are references generated automatically when importing an article from [http://en.wikipedia.org Wikipedia].''</blockquote>
</div>

When you click '''Cloze''', you will not see your newly generated [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]]. Only the selected keyword will change the color. This will speed up your work. However, if you would like to immediately edit the newly created [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletion]], choose the '''Back''' button ([[File:Back.gif|Back enables you to go back to the most recently visited element]]) on the [[navigation bar]] or press ''Alt+Left arrow''. This will make it possible to add context clues, shorten the text, improve the wording, etc.

==== Simplifying questions ====

While converting [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] into questions and answers, you should make sure your questions are simple, clear and carrying the relevant context. For example, if you have extracted the following fragment from your reading about the history of the Internet:
<blockquote>
''The Internet was started in 1969 under a contract let by the Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA) which connected four major computers at universities in the southwestern US (UCLA, Stanford Research Institute, UCSB, and the University of Utah)''</blockquote>

you may discover that when review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] become long enough, you may not actually be able to recall the name of the ARPA agency or even forget the year in which the Internet started. You can then select an important keyword, e.g. ''1969'', and use '''Remember cloze''' to produce the following question-answer pair:

<blockquote>
'''''Question''': The Internet was started in <span class="cloze">[...]</span> under a contract let by the Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA) which connected four major computers at universities in the southwestern US (UCLA, Stanford Research Institute, UCSB, and the University of Utah)<br>
'''Answer''': 1969''
</blockquote>

In the course of learning, you will need to polish the above [[Glossary:Item|item]] by manual editing it to a more compact and understandable form:

<blockquote>
'''''Question''': The Internet was started in <span class="cloze">[...](year)</span> under a contract let by the ARPA agency<br>
'''Answer''': 1969''
</blockquote>

Or better yet:
<blockquote>
'''''Question''': The Internet was started in <span class="cloze">[...](year)</span><br>
'''Answer''': 1969''</blockquote>

As for the precious information &quot;lost&quot; during the editing, it can (but does not have to) be learned independently with separate questions generated by '''Remember cloze'''.

The mini-editing of questions presented above added the following benefits to the newly created question-answer pair:
# '''clearer purpose of the question''': the fact that the question is about the year in which the Internet began is emphasized by using the red-colored <span class="cloze">''(year)''</span> hint.
# '''brevity''': by removing superfluous information, you will not waste time on information that is not likely to be remembered (only actively recalled material will be remembered for years). You will answer the question and never focus on which universities were originally connected by the early Internet. If you believe this information is also important, you will use the original [[Glossary:Extract|extract]] to produce more [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze items]] that will focus solely on the universities in question by naming them in the answer field (if you disagree, read: [http://super-memory.com/articles/20rules.htm 20 rules of formulating knowledge]).
# '''understandability''': ''&quot;the ARPA agency&quot;'' phrase may defy grammar rules you have learned in primary school, but it is by far more understandable than just ''the ARPA''. In SuperMemo, understandability is more important than stiff rules of grammar or spelling!

=== Skill 4: Repetition and review ===

[[Introduction|SuperMemo is based on repetition]]. You will review the learned material from time to time to make sure you prevent forgetting.

If you have never tried SuperMemo before, you will need to get the hang of standard repetitions as described [[Introduction|here]].

In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], your review will be based on similar principles as in classical SuperMemo. The main differences
are:
* the learning process will intermingle reading of new articles with reviewing your [[Glossary:Item|items]]
* your [[Glossary:Item|items]] will mostly have a form of [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]], i.e. sentences with a question posed by a missing part <span class="cloze">[...]</span> (e.g. The planet nearest the Sun is <span class="cloze">[...]</span>)
* as the entire learning process is incremental, your [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] will often show up in an unfinished form

Incrementally processed articles will be subject to periodic review/reading. When you resume reading an article after a certain period, you will proceed to new sections, extracting newly acquired wisdom into
separate [[Glossary:Element|elements]] with ''Alt+X'' (i.e. '''Remember extract'''). Usually, you will delete the remnants of the processed article with '''Delete before cursor''' (''Alt+\'').

The algorithms that determine the timing of (1) repetitions of question-and-answer material and (2) reviewing reading material are analogous but not identical. Most importantly, all repetitions and article presentations happen in increasing [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] by default. In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], you will see a constant inflow of new articles into your [[Glossary:Collection|collection]]. Unprocessed material will need to compete with the newly imported material. Increasing review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] make sure that your old material fades into lower priority if not processed early. The speed of processing will depend on the availability of your time and the value of the material itself. Articles that are boring, badly written, less important for your work or growth, will receive smaller portions of your attention and may go into long review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] before you even manage to pass a fraction of the text. That is an inevitable side effect of a voluminous flow of new information into your [[Glossary:Collection|collection]] and into your memory. However, [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] and [[Glossary:Priority|priorities]] can easily be adjusted. If your priorities change, you can modify the way you process important articles. At review time, you can either read the entire article without interruption, or bring it back for review in a shorter [[Glossary:Interval|interval]]. You can manually change its [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] (e.g. with ''Alt+P''). You can also use search tools (e.g. ''Ctrl+F'') to locate more articles on the subject that you feel you have neglected. You can reprioritize a bunch of articles by changing their [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. You can shorten [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] of articles, or review them all when needed (see: [[Subset review]]).

The algorithm for reviewing questions and answers (e.g. [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]) is quite complex and limits your influence on the timing of repetitions (see: [https://supermemo.guru/wiki/SuperMemo_Algorithm SuperMemo Algorithm]). This is to ensure that you achieve a high level of knowledge [[Glossary:Retention|retention]], which might be compromised by manual intervention. However, the algorithm for determining inter-review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] for [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] is much simpler and is entirely under your control. Each article receives a specific [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. The priority determines which articles are reviewed first and which can be postponed in case you run out of time. Each article is also assigned a number called the [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factor]] that determines how much [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] increase between subsequent reviews. For example, if [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factor]] is 2, review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] will double with each review. [[Glossary:Priority|Priority]] and [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factors]] are set automatically, but you can change them manually at any time. [[Glossary:Priority|Priorities]] and [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factors]] are determined and modified heuristically on the basis of the length of the text, the way it is processed, the way it is postponed or advanced, and by many other factors. You can change the [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] and [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factor]] of an article by pressing ''Alt+P''. You can also use ''Shift+Ctrl+Up arrow'' and ''Shift+Ctrl+Down arrow'' to increase or decrease an [[Glossary:Element|element]]'s [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. Note that [[Glossary:A-Factor|A-Factors]] associated with [[Glossary:Item|items]] cannot be changed by the user, as they are a reflection of item difficulty that determines the length of optimum inter-repetition [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] (see: [https://supermemo.guru/wiki/Forgetting_index_in_SuperMemo Forgetting index]).

You can control the timing of article review by manually adjusting inter-review [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]]. Use ''Ctrl+J'' ('''Reschedule''') or ''Shift+Ctrl+R'' ('''Execute repetition''') to determine the date of the next review. ''Ctrl+J'' will increment the current [[Glossary:Interval|interval]], while ''Shift+Ctrl+R'' will first execute a [[Glossary:Repetition|repetition]] and then set the new interval. For example, if your current [[Glossary:Interval|interval]] is 100 and you specify the value of 3 in '''Reschedule''', your new repetition date will be set in 3 days, and the last repetition date will not change (the new interval will be 103). If you do the same with '''Execute repetition''', your new [[Glossary:Interval|interval]] will be 3 and the last repetition date will be set to today. In other words, '''Reschedule''' increments the [[Glossary:Interval|interval]] (it can also shorten intervals), while '''Execute repetition''' sets the length of the interval (while leaving a trace of a repetition executed in the learning process). Note that '''Reschedule''' (''Ctrl+J'') executed at '''Next repetition''' stage will first complete the repetition and will have the same effect as '''Execute repetition''' (''Shift+Ctrl+R''). To delay a repetition during learning, use any of the earlier stages of the repetition cycle.

In a heavily overloaded incremental reading process, you will often want to focus on a specific subject on a given day (e.g. before an exam). For that purpose, read about the priceless tool: [[subset learning]].

==== Summary ====

* use the '''[[Learn]]''' button to process, learn, and review all your knowledge
* the review of [[Glossary:Item|items]] is handled by the [https://supermemo.guru/wiki/SuperMemo_Algorithm SuperMemo Algorithm]. Grade your items well, formulate them well, and mark them with honest [[Glossary:Priority|priorities]]. SuperMemo will take care of the rest
* review of [[Glossary:Topic|topics]]/articles also occurs in increasing [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]], however, you can always manually set the next date with '''Execute repetition''' (''Shift+Ctrl+R''). Make sure you mark your top articles with high [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. Otherwise, they can quickly fade from view

=== Skill 5: Handling large volumes of knowledge ===

In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], you may quickly import and produce more learning material than you can effectively process. To make sure that you can swiftly handle the [[Glossary:Overload|overload]], SuperMemo uses the [[Glossary:Priority_queue|priority queue]].

Using ''Alt+P'' ('''[[Element menu#Priority|Priority]] : Modify''' on the [[element menu]]), you can set each [[Glossary:Element|element]]'s [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] from 0% to 100%. Note that 0% corresponds
with high priority!

By default, the outstanding repetitions will be [[Glossary:Auto-sort|auto-sorted]] from high to low [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. This way, if you fail to complete your daily load of learning, it will only be the lower priority material that will suffer. Also by default, at the beginning of your working day (i.e. at your first run of SuperMemo), your outstanding material from previous days will be be [[Glossary:Auto-postpone|auto-postponed]] (again with high-priority material being least affected).

Read an article about the [[Glossary:Priority_queue|priority queue]] to learn more about:

# manual sorting of [[Glossary:Element|elements]],
# defining sorting criteria,
# turning off [[Glossary:Auto-sort|auto-sort]] and [[Glossary:Auto-postpone|auto-postpone]], and more.

For more options for handling the overload, see:

* [[Postpone|the postpone dialog]] to postpone portions of the learning material and to define the postpone criteria
* [[Mercy]] to spread the excess of the learning material over a period of time (or to advance the material before a vacation, etc.)
* to learn more about different options, see also: [[Postpone, Advance and Mercy]]

== Other basic skills ==

=== Evolution of knowledge in incremental reading ===

3 main principles will underlie the evolution of knowledge in SuperMemo:

* '''decrease in complexity''' - articles will be converted into sets of paragraphs. Paragraphs will be dismantled into sets of independent sentences and statements. Sentences will be shortened to maximize the information-vs-wording ratio, etc.
* '''[[Glossary:Active_recall|active recall]]''' - all pieces of information will ultimately be converted into '''active recall''' material such as question-answer pairs, [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]], picture recognition tests, sound recognition tests, etc. This is to maximize your recall of knowledge
* '''incrementalism''' - all changes will take place gradually in proportion to available time, with respect to your selected material's priority, and in line with the gradually increasing strength of memory traces. Incremental nature of learning in SuperMemo will help you get the maximum memory effect in minimum time. See: [[Incremental learning#The value of interruption in learning|The value of interruption in learning]]

=== Using pictures ===

For additional information, mnemonic cues, and a sheer fun of learning, an article that you read incrementally in SuperMemo can be illustrated with meaningful pictures taken from its contents, or from other sources. Press ''Ctrl+F8'' to choose one of the pictures embedded in the article.

<div class="center">
[[Image:Incremental reading Topic.jpg|800px|SuperMemo: A topic with an article about the greenhouse effect imported from Wikipedia]]
</div>

If you happen to import from [http://en.wikipedia.org Wikipedia], SuperMemo 16 or later makes it possible to illustrate an article and all its [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] with full resolution images instead of just thumbs.

<div class="center">
[[File:Download_images.jpg|771px|SuperMemo: Download images dialog box makes it possible for you to get images embedded in local pages imported from the net and put them to the image registry]]
{{:File:Download images.jpg}}
</div>

For more, see: [[Visual learning]]

=== Topics vs. Items ===

In SuperMemo you see pieces of information presented to you in 2 basic forms:

* '''topics''': these are usually longer articles that you want to read 
* '''items''': these are usually specific questions that you will need to answer

[[Glossary:Topic|Topics]] and [[Glossary:Item|items]] are presented in a different manner and at different times. '''[[Glossary:Topic|Topics]] keep the knowledge you want to learn''' (i.e. things you want to read about), while '''[[Glossary:Item|items]] keep the knowledge that you want to remember''' (i.e. the knowledge you already posses, but might forget).

==== Topics ====

A topic in SuperMemo is an article, its part, or a sentence that you want to learn. Topics can also have a form of a picture, a video, a piece of music, etc. Unlike [[Glossary:Item|items]], topics do not test your knowledge. They are used in [[Glossary:Passive_review|passive reading]], watching, or listening only. Short text topics are used to generate [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]. Topics take part in the incremental learning process. Once they are converted to [[Glossary:Item|items]], they are often [[Glossary:Dismiss|dismissed]] (i.e. ignored in learning) or [[Element menu#Done|done]] (i.e. deleted from the learning process altogether). Both '''Done!''' and '''Dismiss''' must be executed by the user (i.e. they are not automatic).

===== Using topics =====

Topics are marked in '''[[Contents]]''' with a green T icon ([[File:Topic memorized.gif|A topic taking part in the learning process]]). Topics may be very long (entire articles) or very short (single sentences). This is how you work with topics:

* read the topic from the top
* if you find some interesting information, extract it (e.g. with ''Alt+X''); the extract will form a new independent topic; the new topic will be shorter and will be handled in the same way as all other topics
* decide how far you want to go into reading the topic depending on its priority and available time (e.g. interrupt fast, if you are in a hurry, or read it all, if the topic is of top importance)
* if you finish reading the topic, execute '''Done!''' (e.g. ''Ctrl+Shift+Enter''); this will delete the topic without deleting the material that it produced
* only if the topic is as short as a single sentence, create [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] (e.g. with ''Alt+Z'')
* return to reading the topic next time it comes for review

On longer topics you '''read and extract''', on very short topics you '''generate [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]'''.

==== Items ====

Item in SuperMemo is a piece of knowledge that you want to remember. It usually has a question&amp;answer form. The main difference between an item and a [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] is that an item actively tests your memory (e.g. with a question), while a topic is used for [[Glossary:Passive_review|passive review]] only (e.g. for reading, viewing, watching, etc.).

==== Concepts ====

SuperMemo 17 introduced a new element type: concept (denoted in '''[[Contents]]''' with an orange lightbulb icon ([[Image:Concept memorized.gif|SuperMemo: A concept taking part in the learning process]])). Such an [[Glossary:Element|element]] represents an important idea or subject. Multiple [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] and [[Glossary:Item|items]] (or even [[Glossary:Task|tasks]]) can be linked to a concept. The link associates them with the idea/subject represented by the concept. The concept-based network of links is called a '''concept map'''. It forms a skeleton for the [[Glossary:Spreading_activation|spreading activation]] which underlies [[Glossary:Neural_review|neural review]].

==== Tasks ====

In addition to [[Glossary:Item|items]], [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] and [[Glossary:Concept|concepts]], you can also use tasks in incremental learning. Tasks are jobs sorted by Value/Time or Value/Price ratio.

For an extensive comparison of [[Glossary:Item|items]], [[Glossary:Topic|topics]], [[Glossary:Concept|concepts]] and [[Glossary:Task|tasks]] in SuperMemo, see: [[Items, topics, concepts, and tasks|Element types in SuperMemo]].

=== Reading overload ===

''Overload'' occurs when the student has more [[Glossary:Outstanding_material|outstanding]] [[Glossary:Item|items]] or [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] to review than (s)he can handle. Few users can sustain more than 200 item repetitions per day. When the '''[[Statistics#Outstanding|Outstanding]]''' parameter in the [[Statistics|'''Statistics''' window]] starts going above that number, ''overload'' is likely.

Overload can best be handled with '''[[Glossary:Auto-postpone|Auto-postpone]]'''. However, a one-time big load can be resolved efficiently with '''[[Postpone]]''' (delaying all [[Glossary:Element|elements]]), or '''[[Mercy]]''' (spreading all review in time).

You can also postpone a specific [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] with all its [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] using the following method:

# Go to the [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] in question 
# Press ''Ctrl+Space'' to open the [[Glossary:Topic|topic]], its [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]], and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] in the [[browser]]
# Choose '''[[Subset operations|Process browser&gt;]] : [[Postpone]]''' on the [[browser menu]]

Note that you may need to use '''[[Element menu#Learning|Learning]] : Locate extracts''' on the [[element menu]] if you have moved portions of your learning material to other [[Glossary:Branch|branches]].

See also:
* [[Priority queue]]
* [[Mercy]]
* [[Postpone]]

=== Auto-sort and auto-postpone ===

As long as you prioritize your learning material well, you should make your life easier by checking the following 2 options:

* '''[[Learn menu|Learn]] : [[Learn menu#Sorting|Sorting]] : Auto-sort repetitions''' that results in sorting your [[Glossary:Outstanding_queue|outstanding queue]] by [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] at the start of each day.
* '''[[Learn menu|Learn]] : [[Learn menu#Postpone|Postpone]] : Auto-postpone''' that results in postponing [[Glossary:Outstanding_material|outstanding repetitions]] of lower [[Glossary:Priority|priority]] at that start of each day. It ensures you do not get overloaded, and it ensures that you minimize delays for top priority material.

'''Auto-postpone''' always leaves a number of top-priority [[Glossary:Element|elements]] in the queue. The purpose of the postpone is to get rid of the main mass of low-priority material and focus on top-priority material. You are most likely to use '''[[Postpone]]''' after a day of learning, while '''Auto-postpone''' is executed before your learning day begins. This is why it never affects today's material, and does not postpone top-priority material from previous days. If you have '''[[Learn menu#Postpone|Auto-postpone]]''' checked on the menu, you will always start the day with all the repetitions scheduled for that day, and a number of unexecuted top-priority repetitions from previous days. Even though '''Auto-postpone''' increases the [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] and reduces the [[Glossary:Retention|retention]] of low-priority material, it also makes you benefit from the [[Glossary:Spacing_effect|spacing effect]]. Research shows that longer [[Glossary:Interval|intervals]] may paradoxically increase the speed of learning (up to a certain point). This comes from the fact that the default [[Glossary:Retention|retention]] in SuperMemo (around 95%) is higher than the retention that delivers the largest number of [[Glossary:Item|items]] remembered per unit of time invested.

You can start with default settings of the [[Priority queue#Sorting repetitions|sorting criteria]], however, if you feel you make insufficient progress with [[Glossary:Item|items]] (e.g. high [[Glossary:Forgetting_index|forgetting index]]), you can reduce the proportion of [[Glossary:Topic|topics]]. If the inflow of new material is too slow, you can increase the proportion of [[Glossary:Topic|topics]]. If your [[Glossary:Priority|priorities]] are imperfect, increase the degree of randomization. If you think you miss too many high priority [[Glossary:Item|items]] (see: '''[[Toolkit menu|Toolkit]] : [[Toolkit menu#Statistics|Statistics]] : [[Analysis]] : [[Analysis#Use|Use]] : Priority protection''' from the [[main menu]]), reduce the randomization. By trial and error, you will arrive at your optimum. Even after you find your optimum, keep experimenting with different randomization and topic levels. This will help you avoid various cognitive biases that develop through the routine of learning. It may also be helpful to execute random review from time to time (just to get a general feel of your overall progress).

With '''[[Glossary:Auto-sort|Auto-sort]]''' and '''[[Glossary:Auto-postpone|Auto-postpone]]''', you will nearly never have to worry about [[Glossary:Overload|material overload]]. Each time you start SuperMemo for the first time on a given day, it will first postpone repetitions that you failed to execute on previous days. It will use default postpone criteria which you can always modify (e.g. with '''[[Learn menu|Learn]] : [[Learn menu#Postpone|Postpone]] : All elements'''). After postponing the backlog of repetitions, SuperMemo will sort today's repetitions and those that were left outstanding by '''Auto-postpone'''. '''Auto-sort''' will use [[Priority queue#Sorting repetitions|sorting criteria]] specified earlier with '''[[Learn menu|Learn]] : [[Learn menu#Sorting|Sorting]] : Sorting criteria'''.

With '''[[Glossary:Auto-postpone|Auto-postpone]]''' and '''[[Glossary:Auto-sort|Auto-sort]]''', you can always begin your day with a manageable portion of material sorted by [[Glossary:Priority|priority]]. Your learning sequence will be optimized with no action on your part (i.e. no options to choose, and no keys to press).

==== Overload hints ====

* With or without '''[[Glossary:Auto-postpone|Auto-postpone]]''', your only sure remedy against forgetting is always the same: '''complete your repetitions!'''
* '''[[Glossary:Auto-postpone|Auto-postpone]]''' affects all days '''except''' for today. If you have low-priority [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] scheduled for today, '''Auto-postpone''' will delay them only tomorrow and only if you do not review them today. This is to ensure that low-priority [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] also have a chance to enter repetitions as determined by your '''Randomization/Prioritization''' balance in the [[Priority queue#Sorting repetitions|sorting criteria]]
* In the '''[[Postpone]]''' dialog, '''Skip the following number of top priority elements''' skips only [[Glossary:Element|elements]] that were skipped by '''[[Postpone#Skip_conditions|Skip conditions]]''' on the [[Postpone#Parameters tab|'''Parameters''' tab]]. It will not protect [[Glossary:Element|elements]] from being postponed if they are not protected by the [[Postpone#Parameters tab|postpone criteria]]. Whatever the value of this parameter, you can still have all your [[Glossary:Element|elements]] postponed. You can best view it as a pro-postpone parameter that is used to force extra postpones (not an anti-postpone parameter that protects your from extra postpones). ''Skip'' here means &quot;''skip postpone protections''&quot; not &quot;''skip postpones''&quot;
* '''Simulate''' in '''[[Postpone]]''' can be used to tell you how well your current [[Postpone#Parameters tab|postpone criteria]] work. It ignores '''Skip the following number of top priority elements''' because this parameter needs no simulation (it will always enforce skipping the said number of elements protected from '''Postpone''' by the [[Postpone#Parameters tab|postpone criteria]])

=== Subset review ===

''Subset review'' is a review of a portion of the learning material (e.g. before an exam). The portion may be identified with [[Find elements|search]], by branch selection in '''[[Contents]]''', by [[Concepts|concept group]], and other means that determine a subset of elements. The reviewed subset material may be sorted by its sequence in the [[Glossary:Knowledge_tree|knowledge tree]] ('''[[Contents]]'''), [[Glossary:Priority|priority]], [[Glossary:Difficulty|difficulty]], [[Glossary:Interval|interval]], recency, etc.

For more, see [[Subset learning]]

== Hints and tips ==

=== Importing articles ===

* Importing articles from [http://wikipedia.org Wikipedia] is easiest:
** to search for [http://en.wikipedia.org Wikipedia] articles press ''Ctrl+F3'', type in some keywords, choose '''Wikipedia''', press ''Enter''
** to search for an article on a subject you are reading about, select a portion of text and press ''Ctrl+F3''. Choose '''Wikipedia''' as described above
** to import Wikipedia articles from Internet Explorer, press ''Shift+Ctrl+W'' ('''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Web import]] : Wikipedia''' on the [[main menu]])
* To quickly import many articles from the web, do the following:
*# find the articles (e.g. with Google),
*# open them in Internet Explorer,
*# in SuperMemo, use ''Shift+Ctrl+A'' ('''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Web import]] : All''' from the [[main menu]])
* To quickly search for articles on the subject you are reading about, select a portion of text, press ''Ctrl+F3'' and choose '''Google'''
* To type your own notes in SuperMemo use ''Alt+N'' ('''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Edit menu#Add a note|Add a note]]''' on the [[main menu]])
* If you would like to store pictures locally on your hard disk (in the image [[Glossary:Registry|registry]]), and make them proliferate in [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]] (e.g. show up in all [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] even if the extracts do not include the picture, etc.), then you must import the pictures to image [[Glossary:Component|components]] using one of the following methods:
** to import pictures included in a single article use ''Ctrl+F8'' ('''[[Component menu#Download images|Download images]]''' on the [[component menu]]), select images and click '''Insert'''
** to import pictures from the web, use '''Copy''' on the picture in your Internet browser and then press ''Shift+Ins'' or ''Ctrl+V'' in SuperMemo to paste the picture (if the picture does not paste, press ''Esc'' a few times to get to the [[Glossary:Presentation_mode|display mode]] and try ''Shift+Ins'' or ''Ctrl+V'' again)
** to import many pictures from many articles in Internet Explorer, use '''[[Edit menu|Edit]] : [[Web import]] : Pictures''') and choose '''Local images only''' or '''Page of images''' as the import mode
** to optimize the tiling of many pictures after the import, use '''[[Element menu#Components|Components]] : Tile components''' on the [[element menu]]
** see also: [[Visual learning#Adding pictures to SuperMemo|Adding pictures to SuperMemo]]
* Wikipedia has recently changed from PNG to MathML default in displaying mathematical formulas. To display formulas in SuperMemo, log in to Wikipedia, choose '''Preferences : Appearance''', go to the ''Math'' section and choose '''PNG'''. Click '''Save''' to save the preferences. You will be able to illustrate your elements with PNG formulas and make them proliferate down the knowledge tree with extracts and clozes
* Instead of scanning paper books and doing OCR for the sake of [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], always begin with looking for electronic equivalents. In most basic areas of knowledge, there are multiple sources of learning materials available. There are fewer and fewer cases where you need to do any scanning. These days, you can even be finicky and search for HTML texts to replace your nice PDF materials (to avoid the pain of [[Incremental learning#PDF to HTML converters|converting PDF to HTML]])
* Some texts rich in pictures and tables may be handled with difficulty by SuperMemo (the older the SuperMemo, the more difficulty you may experience). It may be very useful to learn to use HTML filters (press ''F6''). Some of the problems stem from bugs in Internet Explorer that SuperMemo employs to display and edit texts formatted in HTML. This particularly refers to older versions of Internet Explorer (e.g. IE 6.0). It is therefore highly recommended you install Internet Explorer 7 or later to make your life easier
* If you are pressed by exam deadlines and still not too fluent with [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], it would make more sense to do some of your old and new learning in parallel. For example, 30% [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]] and 70% traditional learning. You are bound to make many mistakes in strategy and the discovery process may take longer than until the exam. At the beginning you will have a big overhead cost (strategy, material selection, formulation, learning SuperMemo itself, etc.). It would not then be surprising if your performance on the exam actually dropped at your first try! You could pick a couple of chapters that you particularly enjoy and use them in your incremental learning practice. You would then process the rest of the material using your old methods. You cannot possibly embark on a massive conversion of textbooks into SuperMemo material before you get the feel of how to do it right! It can backfire and discourage the use of SuperMemo. As always, proceed carefully and incrementally

=== Inflow of new articles ===

* SuperMemo uses 2 basic element types: [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] (articles) and [[Glossary:Item|items]] (questions and answers). Those are treated differently in the review process. '''[[Glossary:Topic|Topics]] represent what you want to know, while [[Glossary:Item|items]] hold what you know'''. To better understand the difference, see: [[Items, topics, concepts, and tasks|Topics vs. Items]]
* Keep your [[Glossary:Topic|topics]]/articles in check. Use your [[Priority queue#Sorting criteria|sorting criteria]] to make sure you get a solid dose of daily [[Glossary:Item|items]]/questions in addition to your reading. High topic overload may slow your item flow and damage the [[Glossary:Active_recall|active recall]] process. '''High topic load will make SuperMemo resemble traditional reading where your [[Glossary:Retention|retention]] is unacceptably low'''. You can decide your optimum ratio on the basis of time needed for repetitions. For example, 5:1 [[Glossary:Item|item]]:[[Glossary:Topic|topic]] ratio would probably still make you spend more time on reading than on reviewing. Increase that ratio to increase your [[Glossary:Retention|retention]], reduce the ratio temporarily if you need to do a great deal of reading. If you are not sure, set [[Glossary:Item|items]]:[[Glossary:Topic|topics]] to anywhere between 3:1 and 8:1. Still too hard to decide? Review 5 [[Glossary:Item|items]] for each [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] in the [[Glossary:Outstanding_queue|outstanding queue]].
* '''[[Toolkit menu|Toolkit]] : [[Toolkit menu#Statistics|Statistics]] : [[Statistics]] : Protection''' can be used to inspect your progress in processing top priority material on a given day
* '''[[Toolkit menu|Toolkit]] : [[Toolkit menu#Statistics|Statistics]] : [[Analysis]] : [[Analysis#Use|Use]] : Protection''' can be used to inspect the degree of processing of your top priority material over time

=== Reading ===

* While reading, you can display the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]] in the control bar by clicking its tab ([[File:Read_toolbar.png|SuperMemo: Read toolbar in the control bar at the bottom of the element window]]). The toolbar may be helpful before you learn to use the keyboard to access all its functions
* If you do not like the large spacing between the lines when you press ''Enter'', use ''Shift+Enter''. Remember this trick! Many users struggle for months with line spacing only to discover this precious tip. This tricky behavior is by Internet Explorer, not by SuperMemo
* Once you finish processing the article, use '''Done!''' ([[File:Done!.png|Learning : Done will remove the article from the review process, and delete its contents (without deleting the extracted material]]) on the [[learnbar]] or '''[[Element menu#Learning|Learning]] : Done''' from the [[element menu]] (e.g. with ''Ctrl+Shift+Enter'' followed by all necessary confirmations with ''Enter''). This will clean up your learning process without affecting the work you have done (all [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] will remain in the learning process). '''Done!''' deletes (1) the article, (2) its [[repetition history]], (3) its [[Glossary:Component|components]], etc. However, it leaves the original empty [[Glossary:Element|element]] as a source of reference and as a holder for the derived structure of [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]. '''Done!''' is executed at the moment when you believe you have completed reading and processing a given piece of text. This usually means skipping all unimportant parts and extracting all important parts of the article. You repeat '''Done!''' on all [[Glossary:Extract|topic extracts]] generated from the article. You will quickly discover that keeping the original articles for reference only clutters your [[Glossary:Collection|collection]], increases its size and produces an excess of search hits. Getting rid of the original is usually the preferred action. You can always get to the original article on the net using its reference link
* If you return to an interrupted article in the learning process, the cursor in the text is set on the last-processed text. That text selection is called a ''read-point''. When leaving the article, you can also manually set the ''read-point''' at the place where you interrupted reading. Choose ''Ctrl+F7'' to set the read-point, or click the set read-point button ([[File:Set read-point.png|Set read-point marks the selected text as the point from which you will resume reading the next time you return to the presented article]]) on the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]]
* Highlighting texts automatically sets the [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]]
* Use <b>Clear read-point</b> ([[File:Clear read-point.png|Clear read-point removes the read-point from the currently processed text]]) on the [[Read toolbar|'''Read''' toolbar]], or press ''Ctrl+Shift+F7'', to remove the [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]]
* ''Enter'' is the default key used when progressing through the learning cycle. When a [[Glossary:Read-point|read-point]] is selected and you press ''Enter'', instead of inserting a new line, SuperMemo will automatically begin or resume repetitions. This will also be the case if you make any selections in the text. ''Enter'' will play its usual function only if there are no selections in the text. Although using ''Del'' and ''Enter'' instead of just ''Enter'' in these circumstances may seem cumbersome, you will quickly find this behavior indispensable in learning. If you still do not like this ''Enter'' behavior despite giving it a try, set <code>Allow Read-Point Enter=0</code> in ''[[SuperMemo files#Program files|[BIN]\supermemo.ini]]''
* If you use '''Delete before cursor''', you may be annoyed by lack of '''Undo'''. However, if you mistakenly delete important texts (e.g. when using '''After''' instead of '''Before'''), you can find a temporary backup of the deleted text in [[Glossary:Collection|collection]]'s ''[TEMP]'' folder (the file is named ''Last text portion delete'' with element number and delete time appended). The backup file is deleted only at '''[[Repairing collections|Repair collection]]''' or at '''[[File menu|File]] : [[File menu#Tools|Tools]] : Garbage''', i.e. it will not disappear if you quit the element
* Horizontal lines can be used to split articles. If you insert a horizontal line and call '''[[Component menu#split_article|Split article]]''', the article will be split into separate [[Glossary:Element|elements]]. '''Split article''' is also available from the '''[[SuperMemo Commander|Commander]]'''. To insert a horizontal line press ''Shift+Alt+H'' or type &lt;hr&gt;, select it, and press ''Ctrl+Shift+1'' (or choose '''[[Component menu#Text|Text]] : Convert : Parse HTML''' on the [[component menu]]). '''Parse HTML''' ([[File:Parse HTML.png|Parse HTML removes HTML tags from the selected text]]) is also available on the [[learnbar]]
* Use ''Ctrl+]'' and ''Ctrl+['' to change the size of the font in the selected text

=== Generating clozes ===

* In [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]], you should always strive at converting passive texts into active questions. Ideally, all passive texts should be deleted when done with. All interference from &quot;outside world&quot; makes SuperMemo less precise. Passive texts provide little extra help in learning. At the same time, they provide interference, and should only be used to generate new [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] (or for reference)
* When you press ''Alt+Z'', the currently selected keyword in the current [[Glossary:Topic|topic]] is marked as <span class="clozed">clozed</span>. The newly created [[Glossary:Item|item]] '''is not visible''' (i.e. you will not immediately see the answer nor the deletion brackets). You can see the newly created [[Glossary:Item|item]] by pressing ''Alt+Left arrow''. Use that key to edit the newly created [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] (e.g. to add context clues, shorten the text, improve the wording, etc.). However, if possible, you should do such mini-jobs incrementally, i.e. on the next encounter with the [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozed item]]
* If you want to cloze more than one keyword, before you apply '''Cloze''', you should make sure that the processed statement or paragraph is as simple as possible. You should try to '''use only one-sentence [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]]''' to generate [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]! Some newcomers dislike incremental learning at first. Monster [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] are a chief reason for their negative feelings. Simplifying the parent paragraph to a simple statement will produce simple [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] that will require little processing. Using '''Cloze''' on complex texts multiplies the cost of re-editing when simplifying texts (in all cases where you cloze more than one keyword). If you use '''Cloze''' on a longer multi-sentence paragraph, you will have to put extra effort in simplifying the resulting [[Glossary:Item|items]]. All [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] should be short enough to ensure you read them entirely at repetition time. Otherwise, your brain will tend to &quot;deduce&quot; the answer from non-semantic cues. This will defeat the purpose of learning! By using one-sentence [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] for [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]], you will save ages on repetition time and eons on time needed to simplify [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] and converting them to the final form based on the [[Incremental learning#Minimum information principle|minimum information principle]]. If you plan to cloze only a single keyword along the incremental principles, you can afford less pre-cloze simplification/editing, esp. on the material that still needs more work on its "big picture" structure
* Your work on extracting fragments, producing [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] and editing them should be incremental. '''In each review, do only as much work on the learning material as is necessary!''' Extracting and editing in intervals adds additional benefit to learning and is more time-efficient. Each time you rethink structure and formulation, you hone the representation and &quot;connectivity&quot; of a given piece of knowledge in your memory. In addition, your priorities change as you proceed with learning. At times, you will over-invest in a piece of knowledge that quickly becomes irrelevant or out-dated. The incremental approach will reduce the impact of over-investment. Incrementalism should then be used not only while reading, but also in the follow-up processing and formulation of knowledge. See: [[Incremental learning#Examples: Incremental reading in action|Examples]]. Unless you work with top-priority material, do not generate all your [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] in one go. Make it incremental. Generate a [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] today, and another one at the next review. The incremental nature of the learning process, variegated coloring of texts marked with processing styles, and a complex extract hierarchy seem to quarrel with the perfectionist nature of many. However, the purpose of incremental reading is the maximum effect in minimum time. For that reason, at extract time, you are already forming passive trace memory engrams of the extracted sentence. The optimum strategy then is not to proceed with generating [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]], but to move on to other [[Glossary:Element|elements]] in the queue or to other [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] in the same article (if the high priority of the article justifies it)
* Once you finish processing a paragraph with [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]], use '''Done!''' ([[File:Done!.png|Learning : Done will remove the article from the review process, and delete its contents (without deleting the extracted material]]) on the [[learnbar]] or on the [[element menu]] (e.g. with ''Ctrl+Shift+Enter''). This will clean up your learning process without affecting the work you have done. All [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] will remain in the learning process. '''Done!''' deletes the [[Glossary:Extract|extract]]/paragraph, however, it leaves the original empty [[Glossary:Element|element]] as a holder of the derived [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]]. Once you believe your [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] cover all vital keywords of the statement that forms the topic, you execute '''Done!''' again. In the end, only [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze items]] remain in the learning process. Note that the process of descending from the source article to individual [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|clozes]] may take years. The whole process is incremental and is paced by the declining traces of memory. A single [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] generated from a short sentence often allows of retaining good memory of the entire statement for months. Except for mission-critical pieces of information, you do not execute [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] on all keywords until individual keywords raise questions as to whether they can be recalled individually
* '''Converting to plain text''': Plain text takes much less space. [[Glossary:Collection|Collections]] rich in plain text are faster to back up. You can convert short pieces of HTML to plain text as long as they do not contain formatting information that may be needed to effectively remember the text. In the long run, simple plain text [[Glossary:Item|items]] might do their work better by depriving you of additional cues carried by the formatting. Leave some of your [[Glossary:Item|items]] as HTML and convert some to plain text. After some time you will probably have your own preferences as to which do their work better  
* After generating a [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]], the last character remains selected. On one hand it indicates which keyword has just been processed, on the other, selections make it possible to use ''Enter'' to move to the next [[Glossary:Element|element]] in repetitions
* If you keep getting questions about the [[Glossary:Template|template]] to use at cloze, use '''[[Search menu|Search]] : [[Concepts#Concept properties|Concepts]]''' to inspect the concept group to which you imported the article and uncheck '''Auto-Apply'''
* You can change the appearance of [[Glossary:Extract|extracts]] and [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] with stylesheets. See: [[#Changing the appearance of cloze keywords|Changing the appearance of cloze keywords]]

==== Changing the appearance of cloze keywords ====

This is how you can modify the default [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] style in SuperMemo:

# From the [[main menu]], select '''[[Toolkit menu|Toolkit]] : [[Options]]'''
# In the '''Options''' dialog box, click the '''[[Fonts tab in Options|Fonts]]''' tab
# On the '''Fonts''' tab, click the '''Stylesheet''' button
# In the '''SuperMemo Stylesheet''' dialog box, select the ''Clozed'' option in the drop list at the top; then use '''Font''', '''Color''', and '''Background''' buttons to set individual properties of that style

===== Removing cloze keyword formatting =====

Display the HTML code behind a given cloze text (e.g. with ''Ctrl+Shift+F6''). In the HTML code, replace ''class=clozed'' with an empty string.

{|class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
!
!Before
!After
|-
!HTML
|This is my example &lt;SPAN class=clozed&gt;element&lt;/SPAN&gt;
|This is my example &lt;SPAN&gt;element&lt;/SPAN&gt;
|-
!WYSIWYG
|This is my example <span class="clozed">element</span>
|This is my example element
|}

Your cloze keywords will be formatted in the same way as the surrounding text.

==== Mimic real life situations to combat memory interference ====

Some texts present knowledge in the form that is difficult to remember. Lists and sets are a good example of knowledge that does not stick to memory. Even if you perfectly know the map of Africa, answering the request: &quot;''List all countries of Africa''&quot; may be pretty hard. There are proven techniques that will help you tackle repetitive, list-rich, or boring knowledge with SuperMemo. All solutions are costly at memorization stage, but will pay handsomely in the long run due to lesser forgetting rate. The basic 2 principles are:

# gradually glue individual pieces to your overall knowledge structure
# be as visual and mnemonic as possible

Here are some specific hints:

* '''use a mind map''': search the net for a nice mnemonic picture of the subject studied (e.g. political map of Africa). The picture will provide the basic skeleton for your memory. Like ornaments on a Christmas tree, you will hang new pieces of knowledge on this mnemonic skeleton. Use the picture to illustrate all [[Glossary:Topic|topics]] and [[Glossary:Item|items]] in the studied [[Glossary:Concept_group|concept group]].
* '''do not learn it all at once''': Add individual [[Glossary:Item|items]] gradually at a point when they acquire some special meaning. Add them when they fit snugly with the rest of your knowledge. Add them when you specifically need them or when you learn about a related subject. If you need enumerative knowledge for an exam, cram it using traditional methods, and still continue adding individual pieces in unique contexts later on when you feel they are interesting or important.
* '''associate with stories''': if you ask an expert in the field, you will probably hear that (s)he mastered enumerative knowledge by association with individual case stories. Whatever he or she learned at school was quickly forgotten, but individual cases or problems to solve leave a good and durable imprint due to their uniqueness. Once you hit upon a story that is relevant to your hard-to-remember [[Glossary:Item|items]], try to learn those items in the context of that particular story (e.g. hang the Cameroon up on your [[Glossary:Knowledge_tree|knowledge tree]] only when reading about [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Eto&#39;o Eto's] move to Chelsea). If you encounter cases in the course of your practise, describe them shortly and use them to supply context.
* '''supplement with incremental reading''': instead of formulating all [[Glossary:Item|items]] along the same repetitive and monotonous template, try to use [[Glossary:Incremental_reading|incremental reading]] to generate [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze deletions]] that work on separate storylines. Ideally, you would review your topic and generate just a single subtopic (e.g. on a single country in Africa). Always choose the one that seems most obvious or most important to remember. Always try to add some supplementary material. Be sure you do not provide clues that will make you answer correctly without forming an appropriate association
* '''compare with experts''': ask an expert in the field how (s)he remembers a given fact or association. In some cases you may be dismayed to see how poorly experts recall compulsory college material. At other times, you will see how their memory tackles the problem with ease by using a simple mnemonic. This will help you emulate real life learning at a compressed timescale without ever wasting time on trying to master what others never manage to master anyway. That's the basic difference between school learning and your efficient incremental learning: you do not cram it dry along a rigid prescription. You use your creativity to incrementally build a durable structure of useful knowledge!

===== Example: dealing with enumerations =====

If you happen to learn the geological periods, you are bound to generate nasty [[Glossary:Leech|leeches]], esp. if you are new to the subject. Using the top-down learning rule, be sure you know the eras, before you learn the periods, and before you move on to the epochs, and further down the tree of knowledge.

A typical mistake would be to start from cramming the meaningless sequence of periods. For example, clozing the Paleozoic Era sequence: &quot;''Cambrian, Ordovician, Silurian, Devonian, Carboniferous, Permian''&quot; could result in a question that is bound to cause problems: &quot;''Cambrian, Ordovician, <span class="cloze">[...]</span>, Devonian, Carboniferous, Permian''&quot;. This [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] will trouble anyone who is not privy to the field. In other words, only those who come with the knowledge ready in their mind will be able to tackle this type of question at little cost! '''Conclusion: there is no point in learning lists the hard way unless you already know what you are trying to learn!''' Catch 22!

Instead of using the above approach, it would make far more sense to first anchor the Silurian period in your mind with some meaningful event. For example, the appearance of the bony fish. This way, we might start with a [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]] based on &quot;''The bony fish appeared in Silurian (443-419 mn years ago)''&quot;. The following question will be far easier to remember: &quot;''The bony fish appeared in <span class="cloze">[...](period)</span>(443-419 mn years ago)''&quot;. Even if the answer is the same as in the original unfortunate [[Glossary:Cloze_deletion|cloze]], that question is not semantically equivalent.
